name: The man with the withered hand
notes: A threefold synoptic retelling of the sabbath healing of the man with the withered hand, recounted in Matthew, Mark, and Luke. The shared core runs the same course in each: Jesus enters the synagogue, the man with the withered hand is present, the scribes and Pharisees watch whether he would heal on the sabbath that they might accuse him, Jesus answers with the question whether it is lawful to do good or evil on the sabbath, he bids the man stretch forth his hand, the hand is restored whole as the other, and his enemies plot against him. The accounts give complementary vantages: Matthew alone preserves the sheep-in-a-pit saying (left unlinked here), and frames the question as 'Is it lawful to heal,' while Mark and Luke widen it to 'do good or to do evil, to save life or to destroy it.' Mark uniquely records Jesus' looking on them with anger, grieved for the hardness of their hearts, and that the Pharisees took counsel with the Herodians; Luke uniquely tells that Jesus knew their thoughts and that they were filled with madness. Where Matthew gathers the watching and the accusation into a single verse while Mark and Luke set them in separate verses, the single verse is linked to both.
